title: Graphs of sin, cos and tan, and exact values

screens.prompt: A giant observation wheel has radius $R$. As it turns through angle $\theta$ from the boarding point, a rider's height above the wheel's centre (as a fraction of $R$) is exactly $y=\sin\theta$. After the wheel has turned through $60^\circ$, is the rider more than $90\%$ of the way up to the very top, exactly $90\%$, or less than $90\%$?
choices: More than 90% of the way up
screens.reveal: Less than 90% is right, though not by much: $\sin60^\circ=\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\approx0.866$, about $86.6\%$ of the way up. You'll derive that exact fraction later in this lesson and confirm it. First, the shapes of the three graphs $\sin$, $\cos$ and $\tan$.

screens.prompt: $\tan\theta$ measures the slope of the sightline from an observer standing at the wheel's base up to the rider's cabin. Right at $\theta=0$, $\tan0=0$ (the cabin is level with the observer). What happens to $\tan\theta$ as $\theta$ approaches $90^\circ$ ($\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ radians), the cabin directly overhead?

choices.text: It shoots off toward infinity: an asymptote
It settles at $0$, the same as at $\theta=0$
choices.feedback: That's the value at $\theta=0$, not near $90^\circ$. As the cabin passes overhead, the sightline becomes vertical, not level.
$\tan45^\circ=1$, a completely different angle. Near $90^\circ$ the sightline is nearly vertical, which is far steeper than a slope of $1$.

screens.successFeedback: Right, an asymptote. $\tan\theta=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$, and $\cos90^\circ=0$, so the ratio blows up. This happens again every $180^\circ$ ($\pi$ radians) after that, since $\tan$'s period is half that of $\sin$ and $\cos$.

screens.prompt: Meanwhile $\sin$ and $\cos$ never blow up: they stay between $-1$ and $1$ the whole way round. At the boarding point, $\sin0=0$ and $\cos0=1$ (the rider starts level with the centre, at the wheel's rim). What is $\sin\theta$ a half turn later, at $\theta=\pi$ (180 degrees)?
screens.successFeedback: Right, $\sin\pi=0$. The rider is back at centre height, exactly opposite the boarding point.
hints: A half turn brings the rider back to centre height, on the opposite side of the wheel.
$\sin\pi=0$, the same height as the start, just on the other side.

screens.prompt: Here's $y=\cos\theta$, the rider's horizontal offset from the centre (as a fraction of $R$), with $\theta$ in radians. Drag the point until the rider is momentarily not moving sideways at all: tangent gradient $=0$.

goal.description: Target: tangent gradient = 0 (rider at the extreme left or right of the wheel).

screens.successFeedback: Right, at $\theta=0$ (and again at $\theta=\pm\pi$) the rider is at the extreme side of the wheel, momentarily stationary sideways before swinging back. That's a turning point of $\cos$, just like the peak of $\sin$ is a turning point of the height.
hints: Try the middle of the range shown.
At $\theta=0$, $\cos\theta$ is at its maximum value, $1$, and momentarily flat.

screens.prompt: $\sin$ and $\cos$ both repeat every $2\pi\approx6.283$ radians: one full turn of the wheel. $\tan$ repeats twice as often. What is $\tan$'s period, in radians, as a decimal to 2 decimal places?
screens.successFeedback: Right, $\pi\approx3.14$. Each asymptote-to-asymptote branch of $\tan$ repeats twice as fast as a full cycle of $\sin$ or $\cos$.

screens.body:
Exact values come from two special right-angled triangles, memorised rather than looked up. Take a right-angled isosceles triangle with both legs of length $1$. By Pythagoras its hypotenuse is $\sqrt{1^2+1^2}=\sqrt2$, and each of the two equal angles is $45^\circ$:

$$
\sin45^\circ=\cos45^\circ=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt2}=\dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\approx0.707,\qquad\tan45^\circ=\dfrac11=1
$$
screens.selfExplainPrompt: Why are $\sin45^\circ$ and $\cos45^\circ$ exactly equal to each other, when for most angles sine and cosine are different?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: The triangle is isosceles: both legs equal $1$, so the side opposite $45^\circ$ and the side adjacent to it are the same length. Since $\sin$ uses the opposite side and $\cos$ uses the adjacent side over the same hypotenuse, they come out identical exactly at $45^\circ$, the one angle where opposite and adjacent are interchangeable.

screens.worked:
Take an equilateral triangle with side length $2$ and drop a perpendicular from one vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. That splits it into two right-angled triangles with hypotenuse $2$, one leg $1$ (half the base), and the other leg $\sqrt{2^2-1^2}=\sqrt3$ (Pythagoras again). The angles are $30^\circ$ and $60^\circ$:

$$
\sin30^\circ=\dfrac12,\quad\cos30^\circ=\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2},\quad\tan30^\circ=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt3}=\dfrac{\sqrt3}{3}\approx0.577
$$

$$
\sin60^\circ=\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2},\quad\cos60^\circ=\dfrac12
$$
screens.prompt: So the same triangle gives $\tan60^\circ=\dfrac{\sqrt3}{1}=\sqrt3$. What is $\sqrt3$ as a decimal, to 3 decimal places?
hints: Square your answer: it should come out very close to $3$.

screens.prompt: Back to the very start: the rider's height fraction after a $60^\circ$ turn is $\sin60^\circ$. Using $\sin60^\circ=\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}$, what is that as a decimal, to 3 decimal places?
screens.successFeedback: Right, $\sin60^\circ=\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\approx0.866$, just under 90%, exactly matching your prediction at the start of the lesson. You now have the shapes, periods and exact values of all three graphs ready for the assessment.
